topic: Uncovering Clinical Intuition and Bottlenecks in Early Neurocognitive Disorder (NCD) Screening
researcher_bg: An HCI researcher specializing in Healthcare and Accessibility, focused on designing high-ecological-validity digital tasks (e.g., virtual grocery shopping) to augment early-stage cognitive decline screening.
expert_bg: A seasoned clinical neuropsychologist or geriatrician with extensive experience in conducting baseline cognitive assessments (like MoCA or MMSE) , currently navigating the challenges of unstructured daily task observation and unpacking clinical intuition for early neurocognitive diagnosis.

q_01__shared_root.label: Uncovering Clinical Intuition and Bottlenecks in Early Neurocognitive Disorder (NCD) Screening
q_01__shared_root.description: Interview topic: Uncovering Clinical Intuition and Bottlenecks in Early Neurocognitive Disorder (NCD) Screening

q_01__exp_L1_00.label: Uncovering Clinical Intuition and Bottlenecks in Early Neurocognitive Disorder (NCD) Screening
q_01__exp_L1_00.description: Interview topic: Uncovering Clinical Intuition and Bottlenecks in Early Neurocognitive Disorder (NCD) Screening

q_01__exp_L2_00_00.label: Administration Dynamics & Patient Engagement
q_01__exp_L2_00_00.description: The real-world challenges of physically administering the test and managing the patient's state during the workflow.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_00.label: Rapport-building as diagnostic baseline
q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_00.description: I start with a quick rapport-building chat about their day; if they can't recall recent changes in mood or memory during the chat, the test hasn't even started and I already have my first clue.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_01.label: Adjusting pace based on frustration cues
q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_01.description: If I see them starting to sweat or get quiet during the serial 7s, I know I need to pivot or soften my tone, otherwise they'll just shut down and I'll lose them for the rest of the test.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_02.label: Ensuring quiet environment for focus
q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_02.description: I try to go through the MoCA or MMSE in a quiet room, making sure they're not distracted, because if the phone rings or someone walks in, I can't tell if it's distraction or deficit.

q_01__exp_L2_00_01.label: Clinical Interpretation & Validity
q_01__exp_L2_00_01.description: The mental work of deciding whether the score actually reflects brain health or something else.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_01_00.label: Behavioral process and "in-between" moments
q_01__exp_leaf_00_01_00.description: It's not just about the score—it's about the process. I'll note things like how long they take to answer, if they hesitate, or even what they do when they're just sitting there thinking.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_01_01.label: Probing beyond the standardized script
q_01__exp_leaf_00_01_01.description: The MoCA and MMSE are tools, but they're not the whole picture. I don't just stick to the script; real clues come when I start probing beyond it or when they're not expecting it.

q_01__exp_L2_00_02.label: Contextual & Longitudinal Factors
q_01__exp_L2_00_02.description: Factors outside the immediate test score that influence diagnostic confidence.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_02_00.label: Score vs. Real-World Function
q_01__exp_leaf_00_02_00.description: They might score a 26 which looks 'normal', but if their daughter says they got lost driving home or they can't manage meds, I trust the real-life function over the score.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_02_01.label: Longitudinal Knowledge of Patient
q_01__exp_leaf_00_02_01.description: And honestly, a lot of it is just knowing the person over time; patterns and inconsistencies show up when you've seen them before, not just on paper.

q_01__shared_root.label: Uncovering Clinical Intuition and Bottlenecks in Early Neurocognitive Disorder (NCD) Screening
q_01__shared_root.description: Interview topic: Uncovering Clinical Intuition and Bottlenecks in Early Neurocognitive Disorder (NCD) Screening

q_01__res_L1_00.label: Operational Workflow & Task Sequence
q_01__res_L1_00.description: Examining the step-by-step procedural logic and interaction flow during the administration of standardized cognitive tests.

q_01__res_L2_00_00.label: Administration Protocol Logic
q_01__res_L2_00_00.description: Understanding the sequential rules and branching logic inherent in the test workflow.

q_01__res_leaf_00_00_00.label: Fixed vs. adaptive sequencing of sub-tests
q_01__res_leaf_00_00_00.description: Identify whether the order of assessment components (e.g., orientation before recall) is rigidly fixed or dynamically adjusted based on patient performance.

q_01__res_leaf_00_00_01.label: Criteria for transitioning between test sections
q_01__res_leaf_00_00_01.description: Determine the specific triggers or completion markers that signal the clinician to move from one cognitive domain task to the next.

q_01__res_L2_00_01.label: Instructional Interaction
q_01__res_L2_00_01.description: Analyzing how test instructions are delivered and managed between clinician and patient.

q_01__res_leaf_00_01_00.label: Standardization of verbal prompts
q_01__res_leaf_00_01_00.description: Explore the degree of script fidelity required versus the allowance for spontaneous clarification during formal scoring.

q_01__res_leaf_00_01_01.label: Protocol for instruction repetition
q_01__res_leaf_00_01_01.description: Uncover the rules governing how many times or in what manner instructions can be repeated without invalidating the test score.

q_01__res_L1_01.label: Artifact Interaction & Data Capture
q_01__res_L1_01.description: Investigating the physical and digital tools used during assessment and how data is recorded from them.

q_01__res_L2_01_00.label: Physical-Digital Tooling
q_01__res_L2_01_00.description: Mapping the tangible artifacts and sensory materials required to execute the assessment.

q_01__res_leaf_01_00_00.label: Artifacts for visuospatial tasks
q_01__res_leaf_01_00_00.description: Catalog the specific physical tools (e.g., pen types, paper grids, clock faces) used for drawing-based sub-tests and their affordances.

q_01__res_leaf_01_00_01.label: Visibility constraints of stimuli
q_01__res_leaf_01_00_01.description: Assess how test materials (cards, screens) are presented to accommodate varying levels of visual acuity during the task.

q_01__res_L2_01_01.label: Scoring & Documentation Workflow
q_01__res_L2_01_01.description: Understanding the timing and method of converting patient performance into recorded clinical data.

q_01__res_leaf_01_01_00.label: Timing of score recording
q_01__res_leaf_01_01_00.description: Determine if scoring occurs concurrently with patient interaction or retrospectively after the session concludes.

q_01__res_leaf_01_01_01.label: Data transfer method to EHR
q_01__res_leaf_01_01_01.description: Identify the mechanism for moving raw test scores into the Electronic Health Record (manual entry, scanning, digital sync).

q_01__res_L1_02.label: Environmental Configuration & Accessibility
q_01__res_L1_02.description: Exploring the physical setting and accommodations made to ensure valid test conditions.

q_01__res_L2_02_00.label: Spatial & Sensory Configuration
q_01__res_L2_02_00.description: Examining how the physical room and patient capabilities are managed to support the assessment workflow.

q_01__res_leaf_02_00_00.label: Seating arrangement relative to test materials
q_01__res_leaf_02_00_00.description: Document the geometric setup between clinician, patient, and test artifacts to ensure optimal visibility and interaction.

q_01__res_leaf_02_00_01.label: Protocol for integrating assistive devices
q_01__res_leaf_02_00_01.description: Clarify the workflow steps for verifying and utilizing patient aids (glasses, hearing aids) before starting the scored portion.

aligned_path_expert: Uncovering Clinical Intuition and Bottlenecks in Early Neurocognitive Disorder (NCD) Screening

Administration Dynamics & Patient Engagement
Rapport-building as diagnostic baseline
aligned_path_researcher: Uncovering Clinical Intuition and Bottlenecks in Early Neurocognitive Disorder (NCD) Screening
Fixed vs. adaptive sequencing of sub-tests

conflict.researcher_branch: Fixed vs. adaptive sequencing of sub-tests

assistance_payload.validate_focus: The expert emphasizes that diagnostic insight begins during pre-test rapport building rather than the formal assessment. This perspective highlights how patient engagement prior to testing serves as a crucial baseline for clinical intuition.

pivot.limitation: Focusing solely on the rapport phase leaves the structural logic of the formal assessment protocol unexamined. Without understanding the test sequencing, it remains unclear how the tool itself accommodates clinical flexibility.
pivot.research_goal: The researcher is trying to understand whether the official administration protocol dictates a rigid order of sub-tests or allows dynamic adjustment based on patient performance.
pivot.compelling_reason: Both the pre-test observation and the formal test structure are necessary to understand the full workflow. Knowing the sequencing rules helps determine if the tool supports the clinical intuition established during rapport.
pivot.coarse_scenario: While I see how much diagnostic value you get from that initial chat, I also need to understand how the formal test structure itself supports or constrains those observations once you move past the rapport building.

conflict.researcher_branch: Criteria for transitioning between test sections

assistance_payload.validate_focus: The expert emphasizes that rapport-building acts as a critical diagnostic baseline before formal testing begins. This perspective prioritizes the observational data gathered during the initial social interaction.

pivot.limitation: If the conversation remains only on the pre-test interaction, the structured logic governing the formal assessment sections remains undefined. The researcher would miss the specific operational triggers used during the core testing phase.
pivot.research_goal: The researcher is attempting to map the specific criteria for transitioning between test sections within the operational workflow. This focus targets the explicit decision points inside the formal protocol.
pivot.compelling_reason: While the expert's observational baseline is vital, the researcher also needs to document the explicit workflow logic to ensure the operational protocol is fully understood.
pivot.coarse_scenario: That initial chat gives you a crucial baseline, but I also need to understand what specific markers tell you to move between the formal test sections so we can map the whole workflow.

Rapport-building as diagnostic baseline
aligned_path_researcher: Uncovering Clinical Intuition and Bottlenecks in Early Neurocognitive Disorder (NCD) Screening

assistance_payload.validate_focus: The expert emphasizes that informal rapport-building serves as a critical diagnostic baseline before formal testing begins. This intuitive approach allows for early detection of memory or mood issues during natural conversation.

pivot.limitation: Focusing solely on the unscripted rapport phase overlooks how standardized prompts during the formal assessment ensure consistency and reliability in scoring across different patients.
pivot.research_goal: The researcher is investigating the balance between script fidelity and necessary spontaneous clarification during the scored portion of the interaction.
pivot.compelling_reason: While the rapport phase provides initial clues, understanding prompt standardization is essential to determine if diagnostic accuracy is maintained when moving from intuitive observation to formal measurement.
pivot.coarse_scenario: I agree that the initial chat gives you vital clues. However, I also need to understand how you handle standardization during the scored portion to see how those intuitive baselines translate into formal results.

aligned_path_researcher: Uncovering Clinical Intuition and Bottlenecks in Early Neurocognitive Disorder (NCD) Screening

probes.question: When assessing their response speed about recent events, what pause duration triggers concern?
Regarding the details they provide about their day, which level of precision do you expect?
When they describe a neutral event, which mismatch raises more concern?
If you need to ask a follow-up question to get an answer, how do you categorize this?
For the 'recent changes' they recall, which timeframe carries the most diagnostic weight?
When checking their story, which contradiction type is most critical?
During the chat, which non-verbal cue weighs most heavily?
If they forget something, which reaction indicates higher risk?

hypothetical_scenarios.scenario: If the patient's pause before answering increased from 2 seconds to 10 seconds, would your judgment about 'Rapport-building as diagnostic baseline' change?
If the patient's description changed from 'I had breakfast' to 'I had eggs at 8 AM', would your judgment about 'Rapport-building as diagnostic baseline' change?
If the patient's facial expression changed from smiling to flat while describing a happy event, would your judgment about 'Rapport-building as diagnostic baseline' change?
If the number of follow-up questions required changed from zero to three, would your judgment about 'Rapport-building as diagnostic baseline' change?
If the recall period shifted from events this morning to events last month, would your judgment about 'Rapport-building as diagnostic baseline' change?
If the patient's story changed from having one contradiction to having three contradictions, would your judgment about 'Rapport-building as diagnostic baseline' change?
If the patient's eye contact changed from constant to avoided during key questions, would your judgment about 'Rapport-building as diagnostic baseline' change?
If the patient's reaction to forgetting changed from apologizing to showing no recognition of the error, would your judgment about 'Rapport-building as diagnostic baseline' change?

aligned_path_researcher: Uncovering Clinical Intuition and Bottlenecks in Early Neurocognitive Disorder (NCD) Screening

conflict.expert_branch: Adjusting pace based on frustration cues
conflict.researcher_branch: Fixed vs. adaptive sequencing of sub-tests

assistance_payload.validate_focus: The expert emphasizes the critical importance of micro-level interpersonal adjustments, such as modifying tone and pace during specific tasks, to prevent patient shutdown.

pivot.limitation: Remaining focused only on delivery style leaves unclear whether the assessment protocol itself allows for structural changes, such as altering the sequence of sub-tests based on performance.
pivot.research_goal: The researcher is trying to understand if the workflow permits macro-level adaptations in the order of assessment components rather than just stylistic modifications within a fixed sequence.
pivot.compelling_reason: Distinguishing between delivery flexibility and structural flexibility is essential for determining whether the screening process is standardized or dynamically reconfigured across different clinicians.
pivot.coarse_scenario: I see how adjusting your tone keeps them engaged, but I also need to know if you ever change the actual order of the tests based on how they perform.

conflict.expert_branch: Adjusting pace based on frustration cues

probes.question: Is your adjustment triggered more by visible anxiety, cognitive fatigue, or emotional withdrawal?
When pivoting, do you prioritize changing the task difficulty, altering your delivery speed, or modifying the instruction complexity?
Which signal indicates critical disengagement: silence lasting over 10 seconds, repeated refusal to answer, or visible physical tension?
Which physical cue warrants immediate intervention: forehead sweating, hand trembling, or changes in breathing rate?
At what pause duration do you consider intervening: 5 seconds, 10 seconds, or 15 seconds?
Do you soften your tone primarily to reduce pressure, clarify expectations, or rebuild confidence?
Does your decision to pivot depend on two consecutive errors, three consecutive errors, or a specific type of error?
Is the primary goal of adjusting pace to ensure test validity, maintain patient cooperation, or prevent emotional distress?

hypothetical_scenarios.scenario: If patient_state changed from 'anxious but responsive' to 'calm but non-responsive', would your judgment about 'Adjusting pace based on frustration cues' change?
If adaptation changed from 'softening tone' to 'skipping the task entirely', would your judgment about 'Adjusting pace based on frustration cues' change?
If engagement changed from 'verbal hesitation' to 'complete silence', would your judgment about 'Adjusting pace based on frustration cues' change?
If physiological_signals changed from 'visible sweating' to 'no visible physical changes', would your judgment about 'Adjusting pace based on frustration cues' change?
If verbal_response_latency changed from '3 seconds' to '12 seconds', would your judgment about 'Adjusting pace based on frustration cues' change?
If examiner_tone_modulation changed from 'neutral' to 'encouraging', would your judgment about 'Adjusting pace based on frustration cues' change?
If error_pattern_frequency changed from '1 error in 5 attempts' to '3 errors in 5 attempts', would your judgment about 'Adjusting pace based on frustration cues' change?
If rapport_preservation changed from 'low risk of shutdown' to 'high risk of shutdown', would your judgment about 'Adjusting pace based on frustration cues' change?

assistance_payload.validate_focus: The expert emphasizes the importance of monitoring patient frustration cues to prevent disengagement during testing.

pivot.limitation: Focusing only on pacing adjustments misses the structural logic used to determine when a task is sufficiently complete to proceed.
pivot.research_goal: The researcher aims to identify the specific completion markers that trigger transitions between cognitive domain tasks.
pivot.compelling_reason: Both retention strategies and transition criteria are needed to fully map the operational workflow and ensure the system supports both engagement and protocol adherence.

assistance_payload.validate_focus: The expert emphasizes the critical need for a quiet environment to distinguish between actual cognitive deficits and external distractions during screening.

pivot.limitation: Focusing solely on the expert's concern about noise misses how the physical geometry between the clinician, patient, and test materials affects interaction flow.
pivot.research_goal: The researcher aims to document the spatial configuration to understand how seating arrangements influence the ergonomic delivery of the test.
pivot.compelling_reason: While the expert's focus prevents validity ambiguity, the researcher's concern also matters for the interview goal because it reveals how physical setup impacts the patient's ability to engage with test artifacts.
pivot.coarse_scenario: I completely agree that minimizing distractions is vital for accurate scoring, but could you also describe how you position yourself relative to the test materials to ensure the patient can see them clearly?

aligned_path_researcher: Uncovering Clinical Intuition and Bottlenecks in Early Neurocognitive Disorder (NCD) Screening

assistance_payload.validate_focus: The expert emphasizes the critical role of environmental controls, such as a quiet room, in preventing external distractions from mimicking cognitive deficits.

pivot.limitation: Focusing solely on environmental preconditions misses the procedural safeguards needed when distractions occur or when clarity is still required.
pivot.research_goal: The researcher is trying to understand the specific protocols governing instruction repetition to ensure test validity is maintained.
pivot.compelling_reason: While environment prevents many issues, understanding repetition limits ensures accuracy when environmental controls are insufficient or when patient confusion persists.
pivot.coarse_scenario: I agree that minimizing distractions is foundational, but could you walk me through what happens when a patient still doesn't understand an instruction despite the quiet environment?

conflict.researcher_branch: Criteria for transitioning between test sections

probes.question: When monitoring the patient, is your primary focus on eye contact stability, response latency, or physical stillness?
Does the urge to transition come from a sense of diminishing returns, a structured protocol rule, or perceived patient distress?
Are you waiting for a specific failure count, a pattern of inconsistent answers, or a total lack of engagement?
Is the transition triggered by visible yawning, slowed response times, or verbal complaints of tiredness?
Do you stop when the patient sighs heavily, begins guessing randomly, or expresses verbal irritation?
Is the decision based on sporadic high effort, steady moderate effort, or declining effort over the last three items?
choices: Declining effort over the last three items

probes.question: Which signal weighs heavier: avoiding eye contact, fidgeting with hands, or crossing arms defensively?
Are you more concerned with careless mistakes, conceptual misunderstandings, or memory lapses?
Does the transition depend on the conversation feeling natural, forced, or strained?
Do you move on when they never self-correct, occasionally self-correct, or frequently self-correct errors?

hypothetical_scenarios.scenario: If visual attention changed from steady to wandering, would your judgment about 'Behavioral process and "in-between" moments' change?
If your gut feeling changed from confident to uncertain, would your judgment about 'Behavioral process and "in-between" moments' change?
If the failure threshold changed from 3 errors to 5 errors, would your judgment about 'Behavioral process and "in-between" moments' change?
If session duration changed from 30 minutes to 60 minutes, would your judgment about 'Behavioral process and "in-between" moments' change?
If patient sighing changed from never to after every question, would your judgment about 'Behavioral process and "in-between" moments' change?
If effort level changed from steady to declining, would your judgment about 'Behavioral process and "in-between" moments' change?
If hand fidgeting changed from constant to absent, would your judgment about 'Behavioral process and "in-between" moments' change?
If error type changed from careless slips to conceptual errors, would your judgment about 'Behavioral process and "in-between" moments' change?
If conversation tone changed from natural to strained, would your judgment about 'Behavioral process and "in-between" moments' change?
If self-correction frequency changed from frequent to never, would your judgment about 'Behavioral process and "in-between" moments' change?

probes.question: Is your primary focus during 'in-between' moments on visual cues, auditory cues, or a combination of both?
Does your intuitive sense regarding the patient's state arise immediately upon contact, after a few minutes, or only after a specific trigger?
Is a behavioral signal considered valid based on a single instance, repeated occurrences, or the intensity of the reaction?
Do you track behavioral changes second-by-second, in blocks of minutes, or as a general session vibe?
Do you prioritize observing behavior while speaking, while listening, or during silent pauses?
Do you note a behavior when you are 50% sure, 80% sure, or only when completely certain?
Can you hold a behavioral note in mind for less than 30 seconds, 1-5 minutes, or until the session ends?
Is the behavior significant because it stands alone, because it repeats, or because it breaks a previous pattern?
choices: Because it breaks a previous pattern

hypothetical_scenarios.scenario: If lighting conditions reduced visual clarity by 50%, would your judgment about 'Behavioral process and "in-between" moments' change?
If you were forced to wait 5 minutes before acknowledging your gut feeling, would your judgment about 'Behavioral process and "in-between" moments' change?
If a specific behavioral cue occurred only once instead of three times, would your judgment about 'Behavioral process and "in-between" moments' change?
If you were required to log observations every 10 seconds instead of every 2 minutes, would your judgment about 'Behavioral process and "in-between" moments' change?
If you had to perform a complex calculation while observing the patient, would your judgment about 'Behavioral process and "in-between" moments' change?
If the protocol required you to record cues at 50% confidence instead of 90%, would your judgment about 'Behavioral process and "in-between" moments' change?
If you were interrupted for 10 minutes before you could record a noted behavior, would your judgment about 'Behavioral process and "in-between" moments' change?

aligned_path_researcher: Uncovering Clinical Intuition and Bottlenecks in Early Neurocognitive Disorder (NCD) Screening

analogy.target_concept: Noticing when someone hesitates before answering

structural_mapping.logic: Observing the pause or deviation rather than just the answer
structural_mapping.outputs: Deeper insight into capability rather than a binary score

analogy.explanation: The researcher's allowance for clarification and your in-between moments both involve breaking the script; like noticing hesitation, this deviation is where the real data lives, not in the standard score.

assistance_payload.scenario: When a patient stalls on a test item, do you ever break the script not to clarify the instruction, but to test where their confusion actually lies?

probes.question: When exercising flexibility, is the boundary defined primarily by: A) Time limits, B) Patient comfort, or C) Diagnostic yield?
When relying on intuition, is the signal stronger when: A) The patient hesitates, B) The patient over-explains, or C) The patient avoids eye contact?
When considering protocol_deviation, is the justification primarily: A) Clarifying a wrong answer, B) Testing a specific hypothesis, or C) Reducing patient anxiety?
When observing patient_affect, which cue triggers deeper probing: A) Sudden tearfulness, B) Flat monotone, or C) Irritable snapping?
When measuring response_latency, does a pause indicate a need to probe if it lasts: A) 1-2 seconds, B) 5-10 seconds, or C) Indefinitely?
When assessing rapport_level, do you probe deeper when the relationship feels: A) Collaborative, B) Neutral, or C) Adversarial?
When gauging diagnostic_confidence, do you deviate from the script to: A) Confirm a suspicion, B) Rule out a false positive, or C) Explore an outlier?
When monitoring conversational_flow, do you interrupt the script during: A) A natural lull, B) A topic transition, or C) An emotional peak?
When performing anomaly_detection, which inconsistency matters most: A) Memory vs. Logic, B) Past vs. Present accounts, or C) Verbal vs. Non-verbal signals?
When accounting for patient_fatigue, do you limit probing when signs appear: A) At the start, B) Mid-session, or C) Near the end?

hypothetical_scenarios.scenario: If time limits changed from 15 mins to 30 mins, would your judgment about 'Probing beyond the standardized script' change?
If the gut feeling changed from vague unease to specific suspicion, would your judgment about 'Probing beyond the standardized script' change?
If the deviation changed from rephrasing a question to adding a new task, would your judgment about 'Probing beyond the standardized script' change?
If patient_affect changed from neutral to visibly anxious, would your judgment about 'Probing beyond the standardized script' change?
If response_latency changed from 2 seconds to 10 seconds, would your judgment about 'Probing beyond the standardized script' change?
If rapport_level changed from established trust to first-time meeting, would your judgment about 'Probing beyond the standardized script' change?
If diagnostic_confidence changed from 50% certainty to 90% certainty, would your judgment about 'Probing beyond the standardized script' change?
If conversational_flow changed from smooth dialogue to fragmented speech, would your judgment about 'Probing beyond the standardized script' change?
If anomaly_detection changed from minor inconsistency to major contradiction, would your judgment about 'Probing beyond the standardized script' change?
If patient_fatigue changed from alert to visibly exhausted, would your judgment about 'Probing beyond the standardized script' change?

structural_mapping.inputs: Timer setting vs. Visual check of food
structural_mapping.logic: Follow strict instructions vs. Adjust based on actual state
structural_mapping.outputs: Remove from heat vs. Decision to cook longer

analogy.explanation: The researcher treats the test like a precise timer, believing exact settings guarantee results. You treat it like cooking, knowing you must check the texture because the timer alone doesn't tell you if the meal is actually ready.

assistance_payload.scenario: When a patient scores normal but gets lost driving, do you find yourself caring less about exactly how you asked the questions and more about that real-world mistake?

analogy.target_concept: Recipe timer vs. tasting the food

structural_mapping.inputs: Timer settings (Protocol) vs. Food texture and smell (Real-world cues)
structural_mapping.logic: Strict adherence to time (Score integrity) vs. Judgment of doneness (Functional truth)
structural_mapping.outputs: Technically correct cook time (Valid score) vs. Edible meal (Accurate diagnosis)

analogy.explanation: The researcher's protocol acts like the timer, ensuring standardization, but the expert trusts the 'taste' of real-world function, knowing a perfect timer setting doesn't guarantee the meal is actually cooked.

assistance_payload.scenario: When you hear a family say a patient got lost, do you find yourself valuing that story more than strictly following the rule on repeating instructions?

assistance_payload.validate_focus: The expert prioritizes clinical intuition and real-world functional evidence over standardized test scores when evaluating patients.

pivot.limitation: Staying only on clinical judgment overlooks the administrative workflow required to archive the test data itself.
pivot.research_goal: The researcher aims to identify the specific mechanism used to move raw test scores into the Electronic Health Record.
pivot.compelling_reason: Clarifying the data transfer method is necessary because inefficient documentation workflows can create bottlenecks that interfere with the clinical observation the expert values.
pivot.coarse_scenario: I completely agree that the real-world function is what ultimately drives your diagnosis, but could we briefly walk through how you get that score into the system? I'm trying to understand if the paperwork itself takes time away from observing those functional cues.

analogy.target_concept: Knowing a coworker's reliability vs. judging one late email

structural_mapping.inputs: Immediate performance data vs. Long-term behavior history
structural_mapping.logic: React to current signal vs. Contextualize with baseline

analogy.explanation: Both concepts decide what data drives action. The researcher's concept reacts to the 'late email' (current test score), while the expert's concept reacts to the 'coworker's reliability' (patient history), showing why the expert overrides the protocol.

assistance_payload.scenario: When a patient performs poorly today, do you change the test order based on that immediate result, or based on how you know they usually function over time?

analogy.target_concept: Knowing when soup is done by tasting it vs. watching the timer

analogy.explanation: Both concepts decide when to move to the next step, but like tasting soup versus watching a timer, the expert relies on accumulated context to judge readiness while the researcher relies on fixed completion markers.

assistance_payload.scenario: When you already know a patient's baseline from years of visits, do you ever feel the strict transition rules force you to keep testing even when you're already sure?

analogy.target_concept: Trusting a regular customer's word over store policy

structural_mapping.inputs: Test score vs. family report maps to receipt vs. customer history
structural_mapping.logic: Trusting real-life function maps to trusting loyal customer word
structural_mapping.outputs: Accurate diagnosis maps to approved return

analogy.explanation: Both concepts decide validity like a store handles returns: the researcher's protocol insists on the receipt (strict rules), while the expert's longitudinal knowledge trusts the customer history (relationship), showing why rigid repetition limits miss the clinical truth.

assistance_payload.scenario: When you see a familiar patient struggle, do you ever bend the repetition rules because you know their baseline better than the test protocol does?
